cases.title: Robertson Panel Recommendations
cases.summary: The CIA panel recommended mass-media debunking, use of psychologists and Walt Disney/media expertise, and surveillance of civilian UFO groups.
cases.official: The panel framed public UFO interest as a national-security and communication problem.
cases.gap: Two records in the file remain access-restricted; recommendation does not alone prove full implementation.
sources: CIA-UAP-002 · Robertson Panel report, 42-page declassified CIA file
cases.coordinateBasis: Washington, DC
cases.keyFact: The CIA did not merely study public UFO interest; the panel recommended an organized debunking and monitoring strategy using mass media, psychologists, and civilian-group surveillance.
cases.whyItMatters: It documents narrative-management recommendations in primary-source form. That weakens any claim that public ridicule was purely organic.
cases.keyQuote: strip UFOs of their aura of mystery
cases.heroFact: The CIA panel recommended organized UFO debunking and monitoring using mass media, psychologists, and civilian-group surveillance.

cases.title: Condon Committee Pre-Judgment Record
cases.summary: Internal statements recorded near-zero expectation of finding a saucer before the study was complete, while the final report still contained difficult cases.
cases.official: The Condon Report was later used to support closing Project Blue Book.
cases.gap: The relationship between internal pre-judgment, case analysis, and the public conclusion warrants explicit source comparison.
sources: CONDON-1966 · Robert Low memo / Condon Committee pre-judgment record
DOW-UAP-D092 · DAF Project Blue Book review / Condon context
cases.coordinateBasis: University of Colorado/Boulder institutional centroid; lower-48 calibrated to embedded Atlas USA geometry
cases.keyFact: The Condon study carried internal pre-judgment language before publication, yet the report still contained difficult cases and was later used to close Project Blue Book.
cases.whyItMatters: It exposes a credibility problem at the exact study used to justify shutting down the Air Force public UFO investigation.
cases.keyQuote: The trick would be, I think, to describe the project so that, to the public, it would appear a totally objective study but, to the scientific community, would present the image of a group of non-believers trying their best to be objective but having an almost zero expectation of finding a saucer.
cases.heroFact: The Condon study carried pre-judgment language before publication, yet still became the basis for closing Project Blue Book.

cases.whyItMatters: It is a rare case where an official debunking-era report preserved, rather than erased, a hard residual radar-visual case.

cases.whyItMatters: Nuclear missile reliability, security response, and witness-linked UAP claims converge here; the missing records are exactly the records that would settle the dispute.
cases.heroFact: The unresolved question is whether UAP witness claims intersected with nuclear command-and-control records still incomplete in public.

cases.keyQuote: The many rumors regarding the flying disc became a reality yesterday…
cases.quoteSource: 509th Bomb Group press release, via Roswell Daily Record, July 8, 1947
cases.coordinateBasis: Commonly cited Foster ranch debris-field area southeast of Corona, NM
cases.title: Roswell Army Air Field Debris Recovery
cases.location: Corona debris field / Roswell AAF, New Mexico
cases.summary: On July 8, 1947 the 509th Bomb Group publicly announced recovery of a 'flying disc' from a ranch northwest of Roswell; within hours the Army reversed to a weather-balloon explanation. USAF reports in 1994 and 1997 attributed the debris to Project Mogul balloon trains and later dummy drops.
cases.keyFact: The military itself issued, then retracted within a day, a press release announcing recovery of a flying disc — both the release and the reversal are undisputed public record.
cases.official: USAF (1994, 1997): debris came from a classified Project Mogul balloon array; later body reports attributed to anthropomorphic test dummies from the 1950s.
cases.gap: Original debris chain-of-custody paperwork and Mogul flight logs cleanly matching the recovery dates have never been published in one authenticated set.
cases.whyItMatters: Roswell is the foundation case for every recovery narrative that followed; its evidentiary lesson is that a one-day official reversal, left unexplained for 47 years, permanently priced institutional credibility.
sources: ROSWELL-1947 · RAAF press release & retraction / USAF 1994-1997 report trail
cases.heroFact: The military itself issued, then retracted within a day, a press release announcing recovery of a flying disc — both the release and the reversal are undisputed public record.

cases.keyQuote: …like a saucer if you skip it across the water.
cases.coordinateBasis: Mount Rainier summit coordinate; sighting reported from private aircraft nearby
cases.title: Kenneth Arnold Mount Rainier Sighting
cases.summary: Private pilot Kenneth Arnold reported nine crescent-shaped objects flying in formation past Mount Rainier at speeds he computed above anything then flying. Press paraphrase of his description coined 'flying saucer' and opened the modern era; the report fed directly into Project Sign-era files.
cases.keyFact: The report that named the phenomenon: every 'flying saucer' file after June 1947 descends from press coverage of Arnold's description.
cases.official: Later Air Force reviews offered mirage and bird-flock candidates; no contemporary radar corroboration was established either way.
cases.gap: No instrument record exists; the case rests entirely on one experienced observer's estimate of distance and speed.
cases.whyItMatters: It fixes the start of the modern record and shows how a single credible-witness report, amplified by press language, set the frame every later case was read through.
sources: ARNOLD-1947 · press interviews / Project Sign-era file trail
cases.heroFact: The report that named the phenomenon: every 'flying saucer' file after June 1947 descends from press coverage of Arnold's description.

cases.quoteSource: Maj. Gen. John Samford, USAF press conference, July 29, 1952
cases.coordinateBasis: District of Columbia centroid-adjusted public coordinate
cases.title: Washington D.C. Radar Flap
cases.summary: Across two July 1952 weekends, radar at Washington National and Andrews AFB tracked unknown targets over the capital with concurrent visual reports and jet interceptor scrambles. The Air Force answered with its largest press conference since World War II.
cases.keyFact: Unknown radar targets over the U.S. capital forced jet scrambles and the largest USAF press conference since WWII — the flap that precipitated the Robertson Panel.
cases.official: Samford and Project Blue Book attributed the returns to temperature inversion and anomalous propagation; controllers and some intercept pilots disputed that reading on the record.
cases.gap: Full correlation of National and Andrews radar tracks with intercept gun-camera film and controller logs has never been published.
cases.whyItMatters: It is the clearest 1950s example of simultaneous multi-site radar plus visual reports over defended airspace, and the direct trigger for the CIA-convened Robertson Panel in the atlas.
sources: DC-FLAP-1952 · Blue Book case files / Samford press conference record
cases.heroFact: Unknown radar targets over the U.S. capital forced jet scrambles and the largest USAF press conference since WWII — the flap that precipitated the Robertson Panel.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Betty & Barney Hill Encounter
cases.summary: The first widely publicized American abduction account. The Hills reported a pacing object on Route 3; a contemporaneous report was logged through Pease AFB channels. The abduction narrative itself emerged later under hypnosis, which is not treated as evidentiary.
cases.keyFact: The template abduction case: a contemporaneous sighting report exists on paper, while the famous abduction content came later from hypnosis sessions — two very different evidentiary tiers in one file.
cases.official: Blue Book carried the sighting entry as insufficient data / probable optical; no agency has treated hypnosis-derived recall as evidence.
cases.gap: The original Pease AFB report entry and the unedited hypnosis tapes have never been published together as one authenticated set.
cases.whyItMatters: Nearly every later abduction narrative borrows its structure from this case, so its split between contemporaneous record and reconstructed memory is the split the whole genre inherits.
sources: HILL-1961 · Pease AFB / Blue Book entry & later interview trail
cases.heroFact: The template abduction case: a contemporaneous sighting report exists on paper, while the famous abduction content came later from hypnosis sessions — two very different evidentiary tiers in one file.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Socorro Landing / Lonnie Zamora
cases.summary: Police officer Lonnie Zamora reported a landed egg-shaped craft with insignia and two small figures south of Socorro; investigators documented landing impressions and burned brush the same day. Blue Book carried the case as unexplained and Hynek investigated in person.
cases.keyFact: One of the few Blue Book 'unknowns' resting on a sworn police witness plus same-day documented physical traces.
cases.official: Blue Book listed Socorro as unidentified; later hoax hypotheses involving university students were argued but never established.
cases.gap: Soil and trace analyses were never published to modern standards, and the insignia sketch chain of custody is muddled across retellings.
cases.whyItMatters: Socorro is the strongest single-witness-plus-traces case in the official record — the benchmark for what a trace case needs and still rarely has.
sources: SOCORRO-1964 · Project Blue Book case file / Hynek field notes trail
cases.heroFact: One of the few Blue Book 'unknowns' resting on a sworn police witness plus same-day documented physical traces.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Kecksburg Object Recovery Narrative
cases.summary: A brilliant fireball crossed six states before something came down in woods near Kecksburg. Residents described an acorn-shaped object with strange markings and a military cordon; the military reported nothing found. A 2007 FOIA lawsuit forced NASA to search records it then said were lost.
cases.keyFact: NASA stated in 2005 that examined fragments were from a Soviet satellite, then told a federal court the relevant records had been lost — the record gap is itself on the record.
cases.official: Contemporary astronomers attributed the fireball to a meteor; the Army stated its search found nothing.
cases.gap: The Army unit logs for the search and NASA's missing fragment analysis records remain unlocated.
cases.whyItMatters: It is the canonical domestic 'crash retrieval' narrative where the official paper trail visibly fails — a provenance hole a federal judge acknowledged.
sources: KECKSBURG-1965 · press/witness record / NASA FOIA litigation trail
cases.heroFact: NASA stated in 2005 that examined fragments were from a Soviet satellite, then told a federal court the relevant records had been lost — the record gap is itself on the record.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Westall School Mass Sighting
cases.summary: Around 200 students and staff at two Westall schools reported a low silver disc that descended behind trees and departed at speed, with a flattened-grass site visited afterward. Witnesses consistently describe officials discouraging discussion; the RAAF file on the day is nearly empty.
cases.keyFact: Australia's largest daytime mass sighting sits opposite an almost empty official file — hundreds of living witnesses, no surviving government record.
cases.official: No formal RAAF explanation was issued; a weather-balloon/HIBAL research-balloon hypothesis was advanced decades later without a matching flight record.
cases.gap: Any RAAF, DSTO, or police records for the date remain unlocated despite repeated archive searches.
cases.whyItMatters: Westall pairs with Ariel School as the strongest mass-child-witness events on record, and its missing paperwork makes it a clean test of archival accountability.
sources: WESTALL-1966 · witness corpus / archive search trail
cases.heroFact: Australia's largest daytime mass sighting sits opposite an almost empty official file — hundreds of living witnesses, no surviving government record.

cases.coordinateBasis: Water-impact site just off Shag Harbour, Nova Scotia
cases.title: Shag Harbour Water Impact
cases.summary: Multiple witnesses, including RCMP officers, watched lights descend into the water off Shag Harbour, then observed a glowing object on the surface before it sank. Navy divers searched for days and reported nothing recovered. Canadian federal records carry it as an unexplained UFO report.
cases.keyFact: A water-impact event witnessed by police, logged by the Canadian government as an unsolved UFO report, and followed by a documented naval dive search.
cases.official: Canadian authorities classified the occurrence as an unexplained 'dark object' report; no aircraft were missing and no wreckage was announced.
cases.gap: Dive logs and any recovered-material records beyond the summary reports have not been fully released.
cases.whyItMatters: It is one of the only cases where a government's own paperwork concedes an unexplained physical event rather than a light in the sky.
sources: SHAG-HARBOUR-1967 · RCMP / RCN report trail, Library and Archives Canada
cases.heroFact: A water-impact event witnessed by police, logged by the Canadian government as an unsolved UFO report, and followed by a documented naval dive search.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Travis Walton Abduction Report
cases.location: Apache-Sitgreaves National Forest, near Heber, Arizona
cases.summary: A seven-man logging crew reported Walton struck by a beam from a hovering object; he was missing for five days during a documented law-enforcement search and reappeared disoriented. Polygraph results across the crew were mixed and remain argued in both directions.
cases.keyFact: The only abduction narrative anchored to a documented five-day missing-person case with six corroborating co-workers on record.
cases.official: Skeptical investigators alleged a hoax motivated by a lapsed logging contract; no physical evidence established either account.
cases.gap: Original polygraph charts and the full sheriff's investigation file have never been published side by side.
cases.whyItMatters: Whatever happened, the case shows how a multi-witness report survives or fails on paperwork: the manhunt is documented, everything else is testimony.
sources: WALTON-1975 · Navajo County record / crew testimony trail
cases.heroFact: The only abduction narrative anchored to a documented five-day missing-person case with six corroborating co-workers on record.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Colares Flap / Operation Prato
cases.summary: After islanders reported beams from low objects causing burns and puncture marks ('chupa-chupa'), the Brazilian Air Force deployed a field team under Capt. Uyrangê Hollanda. Operation Prato's reports, sketches, and photographs were later declassified; a local physician documented dozens of injuries.
cases.keyFact: A military field investigation of an active flap — with its own declassified reports and photographs — that ended without an official explanation.
cases.official: Brazil released Prato files acknowledging the operation; no conclusion was published and the Air Force closed the operation abruptly.
cases.gap: The complete unredacted photo set and the medical records compiled by Dr. Wellaide Cecim have never been fully released together.
cases.whyItMatters: Prato is the strongest case of a state actively investigating physiological-effect reports in real time and leaving the file open.
sources: PRATO-1977 · declassified Operação Prato reports / Arquivo Nacional trail
cases.heroFact: A military field investigation of an active flap — with its own declassified reports and photographs — that ended without an official explanation.

cases.keyQuote: It's hovering and it's not an aircraft.
cases.quoteSource: Frederick Valentich radio transmission, Melbourne Flight Service transcript, Oct 21, 1978
cases.coordinateBasis: Last reported position area over Bass Strait south of Cape Otway
cases.title: Valentich Disappearance, Bass Strait
cases.location: Bass Strait, off Cape Otway, Australia
cases.summary: Pilot Frederick Valentich, 20, reported an unidentified object with green lights pacing his Cessna over Bass Strait; the transmission ended in unexplained metallic sounds. Neither pilot nor aircraft was recovered. The Department of Transport transcript is public and the file was released in 2012.
cases.keyFact: An official ATC transcript records a pilot describing an unidentified pacing object in his final transmission before vanishing.
cases.official: The DoT investigation could not determine the cause of the disappearance; disorientation, staged disappearance, and suicide hypotheses all remain unproven.
cases.gap: No radar corroboration of the reported object was ever published; partial engine cowl debris found in 1983 was consistent with, but not proven to be, the aircraft.
cases.whyItMatters: It is the only aviation-fatality-adjacent case whose primary evidence is the government's own transcript of the encounter as it happened.
sources: VALENTICH-1978 · DoT transcript / National Archives of Australia file
cases.heroFact: An official ATC transcript records a pilot describing an unidentified pacing object in his final transmission before vanishing.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Ariel School Encounter, Ruwa
cases.summary: About sixty children at Ariel School reported a landed craft and small beings at the edge of the schoolyard during morning break. They were interviewed on camera within days, including by BBC correspondent Tim Leach and later Harvard psychiatrist John Mack; adult follow-up interviews decades later remain broadly consistent.
cases.keyFact: Sixty child witnesses, filmed within days, telling structurally consistent accounts that have held up in adult re-interviews — the strongest mass-witness testimony corpus in the record.
cases.official: No Zimbabwean government investigation was conducted; skeptical readings attribute the event to suggestion cascading through the group.
cases.gap: The complete unedited 1994 interview footage has never been archived in one public collection.
cases.whyItMatters: Ariel is the benchmark for testimony-only evidence: no instruments, no traces, but a witness corpus large and consistent enough that explanation attempts must engage it seriously.
sources: ARIEL-1994 · interview footage corpus / follow-up study trail
cases.heroFact: Sixty child witnesses, filmed within days, telling structurally consistent accounts that have held up in adult re-interviews — the strongest mass-witness testimony corpus in the record.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Varginha Creature Narrative
cases.summary: Three young women reported encountering a small creature in a Varginha lot; a wider narrative of military capture, hospital transport, and a related police officer's death grew around the event. The Brazilian Army's official account attributes the sighting to a local man in the rain; witnesses have not recanted.
cases.keyFact: Brazil's most cited modern case: a creature-recovery narrative with named, still-public witnesses standing against a thin official counter-explanation.
cases.official: A 2010s-era Brazilian Army statement attributed the 'creature' to a mentally ill local man crouching in the rain; no military operation records were released.
cases.gap: Hospital shift records, garrison movement logs, and the officer's medical file have never been released to test either account.
cases.whyItMatters: Varginha shows a recovery narrative sustained for decades by consistent witnesses precisely because the falsifying records are withheld.
sources: VARGINHA-1996 · witness testimony corpus / press record trail
cases.heroFact: Brazil's most cited modern case: a creature-recovery narrative with named, still-public witnesses standing against a thin official counter-explanation.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: O'Hare Airport Disc Sighting
cases.summary: United Airlines ramp and flight-deck personnel reported a metallic disc hovering over gate C17 before it shot upward, reportedly punching a hole through the overcast. The FAA initially denied any report existed, acknowledged it after a FOIA request, attributed it to weather, and declined to investigate.
cases.keyFact: Trained aviation professionals reported a disc over a major U.S. airport and the FAA's documented response was to log it as weather and not investigate.
cases.official: FAA: a 'weather phenomenon,' with no safety investigation warranted; United declined to discuss the reports.
cases.gap: Tower tapes and the full set of employee statements were never released; no radar analysis was published.
cases.whyItMatters: A modern daylight case at controlled airspace showing the institutional reporting gap AARO-era reforms were later built to close.
sources: OHARE-2006 · FAA FOIA acknowledgment / press record trail
cases.heroFact: Trained aviation professionals reported a disc over a major U.S. airport and the FAA's documented response was to log it as weather and not investigate.

cases.coordinateBasis: Papoose dry lake area south of Groom Lake, per Lazar's own claimed site
cases.title: Special File: Bob Lazar / S-4
cases.summary: In November 1989 Bob Lazar told KLAS-TV's George Knapp he had reverse-engineered saucer propulsion at a site called S-4 near Groom Lake, powered by 'element 115.' His employment trail is thin (a W-2 stub and a Los Alamos phone-directory listing), his claimed MIT/Caltech degrees are unverified, and element 115 as later synthesized (moscovium) has no stable isotope matching his claims.
cases.keyFact: The most influential whistleblower narrative in UFOlogy rests on a nearly empty verifiable record: one pay stub, one phone-book line, and no confirmable education history.
cases.official: The USAF and DOE do not acknowledge any S-4 facility; Los Alamos records place Lazar at most as a contractor-period technician.
cases.gap: Any payroll, clearance, or badge record placing Lazar inside EG&G special projects would settle the case and has never surfaced.
cases.whyItMatters: Lazar defined the modern Area 51 mythos and the template for every 'insider' narrative since; the file documents exactly where testimony outruns paper.
sources: LAZAR-1989 · KLAS-TV broadcast record / employment-verification trail
cases.heroFact: The most influential whistleblower narrative in UFOlogy rests on a nearly empty verifiable record: one pay stub, one phone-book line, and no confirmable education history.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Special File: VZ-9AV Avrocar
cases.location: Avro Canada plant, Malton, Ontario, Canada
cases.summary: The VZ-9AV Avrocar was a USAF/Army-funded disc aircraft built by Avro Canada, descended from the Project 1794 / Silver Bug supersonic-saucer studies. Declassified reports show Mach 3+ design ambitions; flight tests managed roughly 3 feet of altitude at 35 mph before cancellation in 1961.
cases.keyFact: A fully documented, declassified military flying-saucer program — proof the U.S. really built discs, and proof of how badly they flew.
cases.official: USAF and Army evaluations recorded uncontrollable pitch ('hubcapping') above ground effect; the program was cancelled in December 1961.
cases.gap: Some Project 1794-adjacent propulsion studies remain unlocated in declassified collections.
cases.whyItMatters: The Avrocar calibrates every 'it was ours' hypothesis: real saucer programs existed, left complete paper trails, and fell far short of reported UAP performance.
sources: AVROCAR-1959 · declassified USAF/Army evaluation reports / NARA trail
cases.heroFact: A fully documented, declassified military flying-saucer program — proof the U.S. really built discs, and proof of how badly they flew.

cases.quoteSource: T. T. Brown article title, Science and Invention, August 1929
cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Special File: T. Townsend Brown Electrogravitics
cases.summary: Thomas Townsend Brown spent three decades promoting charged-capacitor thrust (the 'Biefeld–Brown effect'), pitched the Pentagon a disc-shaped electrogravitic craft program in 1952 (Project Winterhaven), and demonstrated flying test rigs at Bahnson Laboratories in 1957–58, captured on film. Mainstream analysis attributes the thrust to ionic wind, not gravity control.
cases.keyFact: The documented root of 'antigravity' lore: real experiments, a real Pentagon proposal, filmed demonstrations — and a conventional electrohydrodynamic explanation.
cases.official: No service adopted Winterhaven; published physics attributes Biefeld–Brown thrust to ion momentum transfer in air, which vanishes in vacuum tests.
cases.gap: The distribution list and any classified service evaluations of Project Winterhaven — and the 1956 'Electrogravitics Systems' industry survey's sponsorship — remain incompletely documented.
cases.whyItMatters: Brown's file separates a genuinely documented research thread from the propulsion mythology built on top of it — the atlas's cleanest example of real paper feeding unreal claims.
sources: WINTERHAVEN-1952 · Project Winterhaven proposal / Bahnson Labs film record
cases.heroFact: The documented root of 'antigravity' lore: real experiments, a real Pentagon proposal, filmed demonstrations — and a conventional electrohydrodynamic explanation.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Special File: Chapel Hill Gravity Conference
cases.location: University of North Carolina, Chapel Hill, North Carolina
cases.summary: The January 1957 Conference on the Role of Gravitation in Physics (now known as GR1), organized by Bryce and Cécile DeWitt at UNC Chapel Hill, was funded through aviation money — Agnew Bahnson's Institute of Field Physics — with proceedings issued as a WADC technical document. A legitimate physics milestone (gravitational waves, quantum gravity) later woven into 'antigravity program' lore.
cases.keyFact: A genuine landmark physics conference, bankrolled by aviation-industry gravity-control enthusiasm and archived by the Air Force — the factual kernel inside every 1950s antigravity-program narrative.
cases.official: The conference produced foundational general-relativity research; no craft program followed, and its Air Force connection was archival, not operational.
cases.gap: Correspondence between Bahnson's institute, the DeWitts, and interested aircraft firms is only partially collected in public archives.
cases.whyItMatters: Chapel Hill anchors the 1950s 'gravity control' era in verifiable history, letting the atlas separate what the aerospace industry actually funded from what the lore claims it built.
sources: CHAPELHILL-1957 · GR1 proceedings / WADC technical document trail
cases.heroFact: A genuine landmark physics conference, bankrolled by aviation-industry gravity-control enthusiasm and archived by the Air Force — the factual kernel inside every 1950s antigravity-program narrative.

cases.quoteSource: FBI annotation on the MJ-12 documents, FBI Records Vault
cases.coordinateBasis: Mail-drop provenance: undeveloped film received by Jaime Shandera in North Hollywood
cases.title: Special File: Majestic 12 Documents
cases.summary: In December 1984, producer Jaime Shandera received undeveloped film of an alleged 1952 'Eisenhower Briefing Document' naming a twelve-member group overseeing a 1947 disc recovery. AFOSI declared the documents fabricated and the FBI's public file carries a handwritten 'BOGUS' across the pages; researchers documented format and dating anachronisms.
cases.keyFact: The canonical provenance-failure case: documents that arrived with no chain of custody, and whose own investigative file is annotated with one word — BOGUS.
cases.official: AFOSI: the documents are 'completely bogus'; the FBI closed its inquiry on that basis. Proponents argue later 'MJ-12' drops corroborate the first; document examiners largely disagree.
cases.gap: The identity of the original mailer has never been established, which is itself the case's defining evidentiary hole.
cases.whyItMatters: MJ-12 is the atlas's control case: extraordinary content evaluated purely on chain of custody, and failing — the standard every leaked document must be measured against.
sources: MJ12-1984 · FBI Records Vault file / AFOSI determination trail
cases.heroFact: The canonical provenance-failure case: documents that arrived with no chain of custody, and whose own investigative file is annotated with one word — BOGUS.

cases.coordinateBasis: Production/distribution provenance: Rocket Pictures, Los Angeles
cases.title: Special File: 'Alien Interview' / Victor Tape
cases.summary: 'Area 51: The Alien Interview' (1997), produced and distributed by Rocket Pictures, presented footage said to show an extraterrestrial being questioned at the S-4 facility. It was supplied by a disguised whistleblower using the name 'Victor,' whose identity — and the tape's chain of custody — were never verified. Effects artists and analysts remain split, with puppetry the prevailing skeptical reading; 'Victor' resurfaced once in a 2008 follow-up interview.
cases.keyFact: Footage whose entire evidentiary weight rests on an anonymous source who withheld provenance by design — content that cannot be promoted to evidence no matter how it looks.
cases.official: No agency has acknowledged the footage; the production company marketed it as unverified, leaving authenticity claims entirely to 'Victor.'
cases.gap: Victor's identity, the original tape, and any transfer records between S-4 (claimed) and Rocket Pictures have never surfaced.
cases.whyItMatters: Paired with MJ-12, the Victor tape completes the atlas's provenance lesson for the video era: anonymous chain-of-custody claims fail identically whether the artifact is paper or tape.
sources: VICTOR-1997 · 'Area 51: The Alien Interview' release record / 2008 follow-up interview
cases.heroFact: Footage whose entire evidentiary weight rests on an anonymous source who withheld provenance by design — content that cannot be promoted to evidence no matter how it looks.

cases.title: Pascagoula River Abduction Report
cases.summary: Charles Hickson and Calvin Parker reported being taken aboard a craft by robotic entities while fishing on the Pascagoula River. Left alone in a sheriff's interview room with a hidden recorder, the two continued discussing the event in evident distress — the case's unique covert-credibility artifact. Parker stayed largely silent about it until 2018.
cases.keyFact: The only abduction narrative tested by a covert recording: deputies secretly taped the witnesses alone, and the tape captured continued private distress, not rehearsal.
cases.official: No agency investigated; Project Blue Book had closed in 1969. Skeptical readings propose a hypnagogic episode; the secret tape remains the disputed centerpiece.
cases.gap: The full original sheriff's tape and complete interview logs have never been archived publicly in one authenticated set.
cases.whyItMatters: Pascagoula supplies what every testimony case lacks — a moment when the witnesses did not know they were being evaluated.
sources: PASCAGOULA-1973 · sheriff's covert recording / witness record trail
cases.heroFact: The only abduction narrative tested by a covert recording: deputies secretly taped the witnesses alone, and the tape captured continued private distress, not rehearsal.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Levelland Vehicle-Interference Wave
cases.summary: Over a few hours on the night of November 2–3, 1957, multiple independent drivers around Levelland reported a glowing egg-shaped object accompanied by stalled engines and dead headlights that recovered after it departed. The sheriff saw a distant light; Blue Book sent one investigator and attributed the wave to 'ball lightning' despite no documented storm.
cases.keyFact: The benchmark electromagnetic-effects case: separate, mutually unaware witnesses reporting the same engine-and-lights failure signature within hours.
cases.official: Project Blue Book: ball lightning / severe electrical storm. Weather records for the area that night document no storm, a discrepancy critics have never resolved.
cases.gap: No systematic vehicle inspection or electrical analysis was performed on any affected car — the physical evidence was driven home.
cases.whyItMatters: Levelland defined the vehicle-interference category that recurs for decades; its one-investigator handling is also a case study in institutional under-response.
sources: LEVELLAND-1957 · Project Blue Book case file / sheriff's record
cases.heroFact: The benchmark electromagnetic-effects case: separate, mutually unaware witnesses reporting the same engine-and-lights failure signature within hours.

cases.title: Coyne Helicopter Near-Collision
cases.summary: An Army Reserve UH-1H crew of four, commanded by Capt. Lawrence Coyne, reported a gray metallic object with a green beam closing on their helicopter near Mansfield. With collective full down, the aircraft nonetheless rose from 1,700 to 3,500 feet. All four crew signed statements; ground witnesses corroborated the pass.
cases.keyFact: Four military crew members under flight discipline signed matching statements describing an unexplained near-collision and an uncommanded 1,800-foot climb.
cases.official: No Army explanation was issued; skeptical analyses propose a meteor plus altimeter misreading, which the crew rejected on the record.
cases.gap: The aircraft's maintenance and instrument-check records from that week were never published alongside the testimony.
cases.whyItMatters: It is the strongest multi-crew military close encounter in the public record — testimony with rank, names, and signatures rather than anonymity.
sources: COYNE-1973 · crew statements / Zeidman CUFOS monograph 'A Helicopter-UFO Encounter Over Ohio' (1979)
cases.heroFact: Four military crew members under flight discipline signed matching statements describing an unexplained near-collision and an uncommanded 1,800-foot climb.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Trans-en-Provence Trace Case
cases.summary: After Renato Nicolaï reported a saucer-shaped object landing briefly on his property, France's state UAP office GEPAN (within CNES) collected soil and plant samples within days. Its Technical Note 16 documented mechanical compaction, heating of the soil, and chlorophyll degradation in nearby plants — the most rigorous government trace analysis on record.
cases.keyFact: A state space-agency laboratory analyzed the landing site and published anomalous soil and plant findings under its own name.
cases.official: GEPAN concluded an unexplained physical event occurred; later skeptical re-readings propose vehicle tires and cement dust, which GEPAN-era scientists dispute.
cases.gap: The complete raw laboratory data behind Technical Note 16 has never been fully released for independent reanalysis.
cases.whyItMatters: Trans-en-Provence is the world benchmark for trace-case methodology — what every landing report should have been subjected to, done exactly once.
sources: GEPAN-1981 · GEPAN Technical Note 16 / CNES record
cases.heroFact: A state space-agency laboratory analyzed the landing site and published anomalous soil and plant findings under its own name.

cases.coordinateBasis: Trindade Island, Brazilian South Atlantic territory
cases.title: Trindade Island Navy Photographs
cases.summary: Aboard the Brazilian Navy ship Almirante Saldanha, photographer Almiro Baraúna captured four frames of a Saturn-shaped object over Trindade Island; the Navy examined and released the images and Brazil's president ordered them public. Witness counts aboard remain disputed, and Baraúna's documented skill at trick photography anchors the hoax reading.
cases.keyFact: UFO photographs examined and released through a navy's own chain of command — with a photographer whose darkroom skill is the counter-argument.
cases.official: The Brazilian Navy vouched for the negatives' handling without endorsing the object's nature; skeptics attribute the images to a double exposure.
cases.gap: The original negatives' current custody and any full witness muster from the ship's company were never conclusively documented.
cases.whyItMatters: Trindade poses the photographic dilemma exactly: institutional custody at its best, source credibility at its most contested.
sources: TRINDADE-1958 · Brazilian Navy release / Baraúna photograph set
cases.heroFact: UFO photographs examined and released through a navy's own chain of command — with a photographer whose darkroom skill is the counter-argument.

cases.coordinateBasis: Mid-track coordinate of the documented 700-mile pursuit corridor
cases.title: RB-47 ELINT Pursuit Case
cases.summary: An RB-47H electronic-intelligence aircraft with six officers was followed for over 700 miles across four states by an object tracked simultaneously by eye, by airborne radar, by ground radar, and by its ELINT receivers as a 2800 MHz emitter. The Condon Report's own staff left the case unexplained; James McDonald's AIAA analysis made it famous.
cases.keyFact: Four independent detection channels — visual, airborne radar, ground radar, and signals intelligence — tracked the same unknown for 700 miles.
cases.official: Condon Report (Case 5): 'If a report of this incident, written either by the UFO or by the aircraft crew, were to be submitted... it would certainly be classified an unknown' — the study's strongest unresolved entry.
cases.gap: The original ELINT recordings and ground-radar plots have never been declassified, leaving the reconstruction dependent on crew debriefs.
cases.whyItMatters: It is the single best multi-sensor case of the pre-digital era, and it sits unexplained inside the very study used to close Blue Book.
sources: RB47-1957 · Condon Report Case 5 / McDonald AIAA analysis (1971)
cases.heroFact: Four independent detection channels — visual, airborne radar, ground radar, and signals intelligence — tracked the same unknown for 700 miles.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Cash-Landrum Injury Case
cases.summary: Betty Cash, Vickie Landrum, and her grandson Colby reported a diamond-shaped object venting flame over the road, escorted by what they counted as more than twenty CH-47-type helicopters. Their subsequent hospital-documented symptoms resembled radiation exposure. Their federal lawsuit was dismissed in 1986 after every service denied owning such a craft.
cases.keyFact: The injuries are on hospital paper and the lawsuit is on court paper; only the object has no paper — the U.S. government's defense was that the craft could not be theirs.
cases.official: All military branches denied operating the object or helicopters; the court dismissed for lack of a provable government instrumentality.
cases.gap: Complete medical baselines predating the event and any Army helicopter flight logs for that night have never been reconciled publicly.
cases.whyItMatters: Cash-Landrum is the only case where U.S. courts formally adjudicated UAP-attributed injuries — and resolved it by proving an absence of ownership, not an absence of event.
sources: CASH-LANDRUM-1980 · hospital records trail / federal court docket
cases.heroFact: The injuries are on hospital paper and the lawsuit is on court paper; only the object has no paper — the U.S. government's defense was that the craft could not be theirs.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Stephenville Radar-Correlated Sightings
cases.summary: Dozens of Erath County witnesses, including a pilot and police, reported enormous silent lights on January 8, 2008. FAA radar data obtained by FOIA showed an unidentified track heading toward the Crawford ranch restricted zone. The Air Force first denied, then admitted, that F-16s were flying in the area that night.
cases.keyFact: A civilian FOIA request produced government radar returns matching the witness track — and forced a reversal of the initial official denial.
cases.official: USAF: F-16s from the 457th Fighter Squadron were operating in the area (after first stating otherwise); no explanation was offered for the radar track without a transponder.
cases.gap: Military radar data (as opposed to FAA civil returns) for the track was never released.
cases.whyItMatters: Stephenville proved modern FOIA could still do casework: civilian analysts correlated official sensors with witnesses in near-real time.
sources: STEPHENVILLE-2008 · MUFON radar report / FAA FOIA returns
cases.heroFact: A civilian FOIA request produced government radar returns matching the witness track — and forced a reversal of the initial official denial.

cases.coordinateBasis: Over-sea encounter corridor off Kaikoura, between Wellington and Christchurch
cases.title: Kaikoura Lights, Filmed Encounters
cases.location: Off Kaikoura coast, South Island, New Zealand
cases.summary: Freight flights over the Kaikoura coast in December 1978 encountered lights tracked by Wellington ATC radar and filmed from the aircraft by a television crew — radar, visual, and film in the same minutes. An RNZAF-supported inquiry attributed the lights to squid-boat glare, Venus, and propagation effects; the crews rejected the composite explanation.
cases.keyFact: The rare trifecta on one flight: ground radar tracks, aircrew visuals, and broadcast-quality film shot as it happened.
cases.official: New Zealand's official report explains the sightings as a mixture of mundane sources; participating pilots publicly disputed it for decades.
cases.gap: The full unedited film reels and complete radar plots were never archived together for frame-to-track correlation.
cases.whyItMatters: Kaikoura is the strongest filmed aviation case of its century and a template for how multi-channel evidence still fails to settle identity.
sources: KAIKOURA-1978 · RNZAF report / Fogarty film record
cases.heroFact: The rare trifecta on one flight: ground radar tracks, aircrew visuals, and broadcast-quality film shot as it happened.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: La Joya Intercept / Santa María Engagement
cases.location: La Joya Air Base, Arequipa, Peru
cases.summary: Lt. Oscar Santa María Huertas scrambled in an Su-22 against a balloon-like object over La Joya Air Base and fired sixty-four 30mm rounds at it; he described the object absorbing or ignoring the fire, then accelerating and maneuvering beyond his aircraft's ability to follow. A 1980 U.S. DIA message records the incident; he testified publicly in 2013.
cases.keyFact: The only named military pilot on record who fired on a UAP — with the engagement noted in U.S. defense-intelligence message traffic.
cases.official: A DIA document logs the sighting and intercept attempt without explanation; the Peruvian Air Force never published a conclusion.
cases.gap: Gun-camera film and Peruvian AF engagement reports have never been released.
cases.whyItMatters: It moves the 'weapons response' question from hypothesis to record: an interceptor engaged, the rounds were spent, and the object remained.
sources: LAJOYA-1980 · DIA message traffic (1980) / Santa María testimony, Citizen Hearing 2013
cases.heroFact: The only named military pilot on record who fired on a UAP — with the engagement noted in U.S. defense-intelligence message traffic.

cases.keyQuote: The situation was the UFOs; the estimate was that they were interplanetary!
cases.quoteSource: Edward J. Ruppelt, 'The Report on Unidentified Flying Objects' (1956)
cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Special File: The Estimate of the Situation
cases.summary: In August 1948, Project Sign staff at Wright-Patterson reportedly produced a top-secret 'Estimate of the Situation' concluding that flying saucers were interplanetary. Gen. Hoyt Vandenberg rejected it for lack of proof and ordered copies destroyed. No copy has ever surfaced; the document is known through Edward Ruppelt's 1956 account and corroborating insider recollections.
cases.keyFact: The founding ghost of the archive: an official document whose existence is well-attested and whose destruction was itself the official act.
cases.official: The Air Force has never acknowledged the document; Ruppelt, Sign's successor-project chief, described it in print without contradiction from the service.
cases.gap: No copy, index entry, or destruction certificate has ever been located — the case is a search target more than a file.
cases.whyItMatters: Every 'the government once concluded X' claim descends from this episode; it defines what a credible destroyed-document account looks like when told by an insider.
sources: ESTIMATE-1948 · Ruppelt account (1956) / Project Sign record trail
cases.heroFact: The founding ghost of the archive: an official document whose existence is well-attested and whose destruction was itself the official act.

cases.keyQuote: UFOs and Defense: What Should We Prepare For?
cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Special File: COMETA Report
cases.summary: 'UFOs and Defense: What Should We Prepare For?' — a 1999 report by a private committee of French generals, admirals, and senior engineers associated with the IHEDN defense institute — reviewed the best-documented cases and judged the extraterrestrial hypothesis the most plausible remaining candidate, urging defense preparedness. It was delivered to France's President and Prime Minister.
cases.keyFact: Senior defense establishment figures of a NATO-allied state put their names on a report calling the ET hypothesis the leading unexcluded explanation.
cases.official: The French government accepted delivery without endorsement; GEIPAN continues as the state's operational UAP office.
cases.gap: This dossier's own OCR-blocker inventory flags the full COMETA text as a missing record in the corpus — the primary document should be acquired and mapped.
cases.whyItMatters: COMETA is the strongest institutional-credibility artifact in the field: not a leak, not anonymous — signed, delivered, and never retracted.
sources: COMETA-1999 · report text / IHEDN-associated authorship record
cases.heroFact: Senior defense establishment figures of a NATO-allied state put their names on a report calling the ET hypothesis the leading unexcluded explanation.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Special File: AAWSAP / AATIP & Skinwalker
cases.summary: The DIA's Advanced Aerospace Weapon System Applications Program (AAWSAP, ~$22M, 2008–2012) contracted Robert Bigelow's BAASS, whose portfolio included studies at Skinwalker Ranch. The AATIP effort continued informally under Luis Elizondo until his 2017 resignation — the chain that produced the New York Times story and public release context for the Navy videos already in this atlas.
cases.keyFact: The funding paper trail is real and public: a DIA program, a Senate patron, a contractor, and a ranch — the documented plumbing behind the 2017 disclosure era.
cases.official: DoD confirmed AATIP existed while disputing its scope and Elizondo's role; AAWSAP contract documents and DIRD technical papers have been released via FOIA.
cases.gap: The full set of BAASS field reports from the ranch and the complete AAWSAP deliverables list remain unreleased.
cases.whyItMatters: It connects this atlas's modern sensor cases to their institutional origin: Nimitz-era footage reached the public through this exact program lineage.
sources: AAWSAP-2008 · DIA contract records / DIRD papers FOIA trail
cases.heroFact: The funding paper trail is real and public: a DIA program, a Senate patron, a contractor, and a ranch — the documented plumbing behind the 2017 disclosure era.

cases.quoteSource: David Grusch, House Oversight subcommittee testimony, July 26, 2023
cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Special File: Grusch Testimony & ODNI–AARO Record
cases.summary: The modern institutional arc in one file: the 2021 ODNI preliminary assessment (144 military reports, one explained), David Grusch's 2023 sworn testimony alleging recovery programs and 'non-human biologics,' and AARO's 2024 historical review finding no verifiable evidence of reverse-engineering programs. Sworn claims and official negation now coexist on the congressional record.
cases.keyFact: For the first time since 1952, the legislature took sworn recovery-program testimony — and the executive's own review office formally contradicted it in writing.
cases.official: AARO (2024): no empirical evidence for claims of reverse-engineering programs or recovered craft; Grusch's underlying interviews remain classified with the Inspector General.
cases.gap: The ICIG complaint substance and the named-program evidence Grusch says he provided remain sealed — the exact gap the file tracks.
cases.whyItMatters: This is the atlas's living case: the same government producing both the claim channel and the denial channel, in public, simultaneously.
sources: GRUSCH-2023 · hearing transcript / ODNI 2021 assessment / AARO 2024 review
cases.heroFact: For the first time since 1952, the legislature took sworn recovery-program testimony — and the executive's own review office formally contradicted it in writing.

cases.title: Special File: Bennewitz–Doty Disinformation Affair
cases.summary: Albuquerque businessman Paul Bennewitz, monitoring signals near Kirtland AFB, was fed fabricated UFO material in an AFOSI counterintelligence operation in which agent Richard Doty later admitted participating on camera. Bennewitz's deteriorating mental health ended in hospitalization; several enduring UFOlogy motifs (underground bases, treaty lore) trace to the planted material.
cases.keyFact: A documented case of the U.S. government manufacturing UFO mythology on purpose — with the operator's on-camera admission.
cases.official: AFOSI has never formally acknowledged the operation; Doty's admissions and released Kirtland-era documents constitute the record.
cases.gap: The operation's authorizing paperwork and full scope (who else received planted material) remain unreleased.
cases.whyItMatters: Bennewitz–Doty is the atlas's contamination-control file: it proves some lore is state-authored noise, which every other testimony case must be filtered against.
sources: BENNEWITZ-1980 · Doty on-camera admissions / Kirtland document trail
cases.heroFact: A documented case of the U.S. government manufacturing UFO mythology on purpose — with the operator's on-camera admission.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Special File: Santilli 'Alien Autopsy' Film
cases.summary: London producer Ray Santilli licensed worldwide broadcast of film purporting to show a 1947 Roswell-era alien autopsy. In 2006 he stated the aired footage was a 'restoration' — a staged re-shoot — while claiming a degraded original existed; sculptor John Humphreys described building the body. No original film has ever been produced.
cases.keyFact: The rare hoax with a confession on the record: the distributor himself reclassified the film as a staged recreation, leaving only an unevidenced claim of a lost original.
cases.official: No agency ever authenticated the film; broadcasters aired it as entertainment, and the 2006 admission closed the authenticity question for practical purposes.
cases.gap: Santilli's claimed 'original frames' have never been produced or independently examined — the residual loose end.
cases.whyItMatters: Paired with the Victor tape, it completes the media-artifact lesson: production-quality footage plus withheld provenance equals zero evidentiary weight — confirmed here by confession.
sources: SANTILLI-1995 · broadcast record / 2006 admission interviews
cases.heroFact: The rare hoax with a confession on the record: the distributor himself reclassified the film as a staged recreation, leaving only an unevidenced claim of a lost original.

cases.title: Aztec Crash Fraud
cases.summary: Frank Scully's 1950 bestseller 'Behind the Flying Saucers' described a disc and sixteen bodies recovered near Aztec — sourced to Silas Newton and Leo GeBauer, con men later convicted of fraud selling 'doodlebug' oil detectors supposedly based on saucer technology. J.P. Cahn's exposés dismantled the story in 1952 and 1956.
cases.keyFact: The original crash-retrieval story entered the literature as a proven confidence scheme, prosecuted in court — the field's foundational fraud benchmark.
cases.official: Newton and GeBauer were convicted of fraud in 1953; no evidence of any Aztec recovery has ever existed.
cases.gap: None material — the case is closed; its value is as a calibration standard for retrieval narratives.
cases.whyItMatters: Aztec is the control sample every crash story must beat: named sources, specific details, national publication — and completely false.
sources: AZTEC-1948 · Cahn exposés / fraud conviction record
cases.heroFact: The original crash-retrieval story entered the literature as a proven confidence scheme, prosecuted in court — the field's foundational fraud benchmark.

cases.title: Gulf Breeze Photo Series
cases.summary: Contractor Ed Walters produced dozens of Polaroids of a structured craft over Gulf Breeze, splitting MUFON's investigators. After the Walters family moved, a styrofoam model resembling the photographed craft was found in the house's attic; Walters called it a plant. Some independent sightings in the area remain unexplained.
cases.keyFact: A photo case that divided professional investigators until a physical model surfaced — leaving a residue of unrelated local sightings the hoax finding doesn't cover.
cases.official: No agency investigated; MUFON's own record contains both endorsement and repudiation, which is the case's real lesson.
cases.gap: The model's chain of custody (plant vs. prop) was never forensically settled, keeping both camps alive.
cases.whyItMatters: Gulf Breeze shows how a single motivated photographer can consume years of investigative capacity — the cost side of open reporting systems.
sources: GULFBREEZE-1987 · photo series record / MUFON investigation trail
cases.heroFact: A photo case that divided professional investigators until a physical model surfaced — leaving a residue of unrelated local sightings the hoax finding doesn't cover.

cases.coordinateBasis: Harbor-patrol boat position in Puget Sound off Maury Island
cases.title: Maury Island Affair
cases.location: Puget Sound off Maury Island, Washington
cases.summary: Days before the Arnold sighting, Harold Dahl claimed a doughnut-shaped craft dropped slag onto his boat in Puget Sound; Fred Crisman promoted the tale. Two Army Air Forces intelligence officers, Davidson and Brown, died when their B-25 crashed flying samples out — a real fatality attached to what FBI records treat as a hoax Dahl himself disavowed.
cases.keyFact: The first 'physical evidence' story of the modern era was a hoax that still cost two intelligence officers their lives — provenance failure with a body count.
cases.official: FBI and Air Force records classify the claims as a hoax; the B-25 crash was attributed to an engine fire.
cases.gap: Crisman's motives and the slag's origin were never fully documented, feeding decades of secondary conspiracy literature.
cases.whyItMatters: Maury Island predates Arnold and set the pattern: sensational claim, physical 'samples,' institutional response, and a provenance collapse — the field's original cautionary tale.

cases.title: Calvine Photograph
cases.summary: Two hikers photographed a large diamond-shaped object, with a jet in frame, near Calvine in 1990. The photos went to a newspaper, then to the Ministry of Defence, and vanished into files sometimes called the UK's best UFO evidence. One original print, kept by retired RAF press officer Craig Lindsay, resurfaced in 2022 via Sheffield Hallam University.
cases.keyFact: A photograph the MoD held for thirty years resurfaced through a retiree's desk drawer — the provenance is now stronger than the identification.
cases.official: The MoD file remains partially withheld; proposed explanations range from a novel airframe to a reflection or staged object in water, none established.
cases.gap: The remaining five frames, the photographers' identities, and the full MoD analysis are still unreleased.
cases.whyItMatters: Calvine is the live photographic mystery of the current decade: authenticated custody, unresolved subject — the exact inverse of Gulf Breeze.
sources: CALVINE-1990 · Sheffield Hallam release (2022) / MoD file record
cases.heroFact: A photograph the MoD held for thirty years resurfaced through a retiree's desk drawer — the provenance is now stronger than the identification.

cases.coordinateBasis: 415th Night Fighter Squadron operating area over the Rhine valley
cases.title: Foo Fighters, European Theater
cases.location: Rhine valley air corridor, Western Europe
cases.summary: Night-fighter crews of the 415th NFS reported glowing balls of light pacing their aircraft over the Rhine in 1944–45, nicknamed 'foo fighters' and reported in the wartime press. Allied intelligence suspected German secret weapons; postwar review found the Germans had been seeing them too. No explanation was established.
cases.keyFact: The modern record's true opening entry: disciplined military aircrews reporting pacing luminous objects — three years before anyone said 'flying saucer.'
cases.official: Wartime intelligence filed the reports without resolution; postwar explanations (St. Elmo's fire, flak reflections, fatigue) were offered, none demonstrated.
cases.gap: Squadron-level mission reports naming foo fighter encounters are scattered across archives and have never been systematically compiled.
cases.whyItMatters: Foo fighters establish that the phenomenon's reporting record predates the 1947 media frame — and that both sides of a world war blamed each other for it.
sources: FOO-1944 · 415th NFS mission report trail / wartime press record
cases.heroFact: The modern record's true opening entry: disciplined military aircrews reporting pacing luminous objects — three years before anyone said 'flying saucer.'

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Scandinavian Ghost Rockets
cases.summary: Sweden logged roughly two thousand reports of fast rocket-like objects in 1946, many over lakes; a military dive search at Lake Kölmjärv that July found nothing. Swedish, British, and American intelligence all investigated — Soviet tests were suspected and never confirmed; a substantial fraction of reports resisted the meteor explanation.
cases.keyFact: A full year of state-investigated 'ghost rocket' reports — with lake searches, allied intelligence traffic, and no recovered object — one year before Roswell.
cases.official: Swedish defense concluded most reports were misidentifications while explicitly leaving a residue unexplained; files have since been released.
cases.gap: Correlation of the Swedish reports with released Soviet-era test records has never been completed.
cases.whyItMatters: Ghost rockets show a modern state running the full investigative playbook — search, intelligence liaison, partial explanation — before the UFO era's vocabulary existed.
sources: GHOSTROCKETS-1946 · Swedish defense files / Kölmjärv search record
cases.heroFact: A full year of state-investigated 'ghost rocket' reports — with lake searches, allied intelligence traffic, and no recovered object — one year before Roswell.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Mantell Pursuit Fatality
cases.summary: Capt. Thomas Mantell, Kentucky ANG, died when his P-51 crashed during a climb after an object reported over Godman Field in January 1948. The later, well-supported explanation is a then-classified Skyhook balloon and hypoxia — but the death made UFOs a deadly serious Air Force matter overnight.
cases.keyFact: The first fatality in the official record — almost certainly a classified balloon plus hypoxia, and the event that hardened Air Force attitudes for a decade.
cases.official: USAF ultimately attributed the object to a Skyhook balloon (classified at the time) and the crash to oxygen starvation above 25,000 feet; early 'Venus' explanations were abandoned.
cases.gap: The specific Skyhook flight record matching the track was reconstructed, not archived — a small but real remaining hole.
cases.whyItMatters: Mantell is the cautionary case in both directions: secrecy (Skyhook) manufactured a mystery, and the mystery killed a pilot before the paperwork caught up.
sources: MANTELL-1948 · USAF accident/case record
cases.heroFact: The first fatality in the official record — almost certainly a classified balloon plus hypoxia, and the event that hardened Air Force attitudes for a decade.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Chiles-Whitted Airliner Encounter
cases.summary: Eastern Air Lines pilots Clarence Chiles and John Whitted reported a torpedo-shaped object with a flaming exhaust passing their DC-3 head-on near Montgomery. The report, backed by a passenger and a roughly contemporaneous ground observation, reportedly triggered Project Sign's 'Estimate of the Situation.'
cases.keyFact: The airline-crew report that pushed Project Sign to its interplanetary estimate — later reassessed as a bright bolide, which is itself instructive.
cases.official: Modern analysis attributes the object to a fireball meteor; Sign-era staff treated it as a structured craft, and the gap between those readings is the case's lesson.
cases.gap: The full Sign case file with the crew's original sketches was dispersed; reconstructions rely on Blue Book microfilm.
cases.whyItMatters: Chiles-Whitted shows how one vivid report can steer institutional conclusions — the direct human trigger for the destroyed Estimate in this atlas.
sources: CHILES-1948 · Project Sign / Blue Book microfilm record
cases.heroFact: The airline-crew report that pushed Project Sign to its interplanetary estimate — later reassessed as a bright bolide, which is itself instructive.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Gorman Dogfight, Fargo
cases.summary: North Dakota ANG pilot George Gorman chased a small white light over Fargo for nearly half an hour, describing intelligent evasive maneuvering; tower personnel saw the object. Blue Book attributed it to a lighted weather balloon combined with vertigo and stars — an explanation the 1948-era files document in detail.
cases.keyFact: A 27-minute documented 'dogfight' whose balloon-plus-vertigo resolution became the template for how pursuit-of-light cases fail.
cases.official: USAF: a lighted pilot balloon, with the maneuvers attributed to the pilot's own motion — among Blue Book's more defensible explanations.
cases.gap: None significant; the case's value is as a well-documented resolved control alongside its unresolved 1948 siblings.
cases.whyItMatters: Gorman calibrates the 1948 trilogy: same year, same institutions — one case resolved cleanly, one probably resolved, one destroyed-document mystery.
sources: GORMAN-1948 · Project Sign/Blue Book case record
cases.heroFact: A 27-minute documented 'dogfight' whose balloon-plus-vertigo resolution became the template for how pursuit-of-light cases fail.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Great Falls / Mariana Film
cases.summary: Nick Mariana filmed two bright discs crossing the sky over Great Falls in 1950 — among the first motion pictures of the phenomenon. The Air Force analyzed the film twice and the Robertson Panel reviewed it; the F-94 reflection explanation conflicts with the documented aircraft timing, and Mariana maintained the clearest frames were not returned.
cases.keyFact: One of the two films the Robertson Panel actually examined — with a timing conflict in the official explanation and a missing-frames dispute that was never settled.
cases.official: USAF attributed the images to sun reflections off two F-94s known to be in the area; the timing reconciliation remains argued.
cases.gap: The allegedly withheld first frames and the original uncut reel were never accounted for.
cases.whyItMatters: Great Falls ties directly to this atlas's Robertson Panel record: it is half of the physical evidence that panel reviewed before recommending debunking.
sources: GREATFALLS-1950 · Mariana film / USAF analysis record
cases.heroFact: One of the two films the Robertson Panel actually examined — with a timing conflict in the official explanation and a missing-frames dispute that was never settled.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Tremonton / Newhouse Film
cases.summary: Navy Chief Photographer Delbert Newhouse filmed a dozen maneuvering white objects near Tremonton, Utah. Navy photo analysts spent over a thousand hours on the film and reportedly judged the objects self-luminous and not birds; the Robertson Panel overrode that with a seagull explanation Newhouse rejected, noting he had seen the objects closely before filming.
cases.keyFact: The Navy's own photographic laboratory and the CIA's panel reached opposite conclusions about the same frames — the clearest documented analyst split of the era.
cases.official: Robertson Panel: seagulls in sunlight. Navy Photographic Interpretation Center's contrary internal assessment survives only in summaries.
cases.gap: The full NPIC analysis report has never been located in released files.
cases.whyItMatters: Tremonton is the other half of the Robertson Panel's evidence base, and its analyst-versus-panel split prefigures every modern sensor-interpretation fight.
sources: TREMONTON-1952 · Newhouse film / Robertson Panel record
cases.heroFact: The Navy's own photographic laboratory and the CIA's panel reached opposite conclusions about the same frames — the clearest documented analyst split of the era.

cases.coordinateBasis: Southern California operating area where the 2019 swarm events were logged
cases.title: USS Omaha Sphere & Swarm Events
cases.location: Off San Clemente Island, Southern California
cases.summary: In July 2019, warships off Southern California logged multi-night 'drone' swarms; USS Omaha sensors recorded a spherical object appearing to enter the water without debris, and the Pentagon confirmed the leaked footage as authentic Navy imagery taken by the UAP Task Force. Later document releases tie many swarm tracks to surface vessels — with an unresolved subset.
cases.keyFact: Pentagon-authenticated shipboard footage of a trans-medium candidate, inside a documented multi-ship swarm investigation — the modern case where authentication and identification fully separated.
cases.official: DoD confirmed the videos' authenticity and referred identity questions to the UAPTF; deck logs and follow-up FOIA point to drone activity for much of the swarm without resolving all tracks.
cases.gap: Sonar records that would confirm or refute the water entry have never been released.
cases.whyItMatters: Omaha extends this atlas's Nimitz thread into the swarm era and frames the modern problem: perfect chain of custody, persistent ambiguity.
sources: OMAHA-2019 · Pentagon-confirmed footage / deck log FOIA trail
cases.heroFact: Pentagon-authenticated shipboard footage of a trans-medium candidate, inside a documented multi-ship swarm investigation — the modern case where authentication and identification fully separated.

cases.coordinateBasis: Naval helicopter patrol track off the central Chilean coast
cases.title: Chilean Navy IR Video (CEFAA)
cases.summary: A Chilean Navy helicopter's WESCAM IR camera recorded an object trailing plumes over the sea in 2014. CEFAA, Chile's official UAP office, released the video in 2017 after a two-year study concluded it was unexplained. Independent analysts subsequently matched the object to a scheduled airliner's contrails — a correction CEFAA absorbed publicly.
cases.keyFact: A government office studied a video for two years and called it unexplained; open-source analysts identified it in days — the field's best documented lesson in method over authority.
cases.official: CEFAA released the case as unexplained in 2017; the flight-track identification (IB6830 candidate) is now the prevailing reading.
cases.gap: CEFAA never published a formal retraction document, leaving the file technically open in Chile's records.
cases.whyItMatters: This is the atlas's calibration case for official credulity: institutional provenance guarantees the footage, not the conclusion.
sources: CEFAA-2014 · CEFAA release (2017) / independent flight-track analysis
cases.heroFact: A government office studied a video for two years and called it unexplained; open-source analysts identified it in days — the field's best documented lesson in method over authority.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Langley AFB Drone Incursions
cases.summary: For more than two weeks in December 2023, formations of unidentified small aircraft repeatedly penetrated restricted airspace over Langley AFB, forcing aircraft relocations. FOIA-released records and congressional attention in 2024 documented the military's inability to identify or stop the incursions — the UAS/UAP overlap in its rawest official form.
cases.keyFact: Sustained, documented incursions over a front-line fighter base that the Air Force could neither identify nor interdict — on the public record via FOIA.
cases.official: USAF and NORAD confirmed the events, characterized the objects as probable small unmanned aircraft, and acknowledged no operator was identified.
cases.gap: Sensor data from the incursions remains classified; the identification confidence behind 'drone' has never been shown.
cases.whyItMatters: Langley closes the atlas's arc where it began at Los Alamos in 1949: unknown objects over sensitive defense sites, and an institution documenting its own uncertainty.
sources: LANGLEY-2023 · FOIA-released incursion records / congressional record
cases.heroFact: Sustained, documented incursions over a front-line fighter base that the Air Force could neither identify nor interdict — on the public record via FOIA.

cases.coordinateBasis: Published public coordinate for the named location
cases.title: Special File: Billy Meier Contact Corpus
cases.summary: Eduard 'Billy' Meier has claimed ongoing contact with 'Plejaren' beings since 1975, producing hundreds of daylight photographs, films, sound recordings, and metal samples through his FIGU organization. Investigations repeatedly reproduced his imagery with small models — the 'wedding cake' craft resembles household objects — and most researchers, including sympathetic ones, judge the corpus fabricated. The following remains global and active.
cases.keyFact: The largest single-source evidence corpus in UFOlogy — and the most reproduced-by-model: volume of material is not weight of evidence.
cases.official: No agency has investigated; independent analyses attribute the photographs to miniature models, and early positive metallurgy claims were never replicated under custody.
cases.gap: Meier's original negatives and the disputed metal samples have never been examined under neutral, documented custody — the standing offer that would settle the file.
cases.whyItMatters: Meier is the atlas's volume-versus-provenance lesson: five decades of prolific 'evidence' that fails the same chain-of-custody test a single satellite photo must pass.
sources: MEIER-1975 · FIGU photographic corpus / model-reproduction analyses
cases.heroFact: The largest single-source evidence corpus in UFOlogy — and the most reproduced-by-model: volume of material is not weight of evidence.

cases.title: Magenta 1933 / Gabinetto RS/33 Claim
cases.coordinateBasis: Magenta township, Lombardy — alleged crash vicinity per the RS/33 document set
cases.summary: Documents mailed anonymously to Italian researcher Roberto Pinotti beginning in 1996 describe a craft coming down near Magenta in June 1933, a secret Mussolini-era study group ('Gabinetto RS/33') allegedly chaired by Guglielmo Marconi, wreckage stored at SIAI Marchetti facilities in Vergiate, and a 1945 transfer to U.S. custody. No Italian state-archive corroboration has been located, and Italy's CISU researchers judge the papers likely fabricated. The case went global in 2023 when David Grusch cited Magenta 1933 as the first retrieval.
cases.official: No record of a crash, an RS/33 cabinet, or a Marconi appointment has been found in Italian state archives; CISU assesses the document set as probable fabrication, and no U.S. agency has acknowledged any 1945 transfer.
cases.gap: The anonymous mailer's identity and any archival trace of Gabinetto RS/33 remain unlocated — a single authenticated 1930s document would transform the file.
sources: MAGENTA-1933 · Pinotti/Lissoni RS/33 document set (anonymous mail, 1996–) / Grusch citation record (2023)
cases.keyFact: A pre-Roswell retrieval claim resting entirely on documents mailed anonymously in 1996 — revived when a sworn-era whistleblower cited it by name in 2023.
cases.heroFact: A pre-Roswell retrieval claim resting entirely on documents mailed anonymously in 1996 — revived when a sworn-era whistleblower cited it by name in 2023.
cases.whyItMatters: Magenta binds the modern testimony era to MJ-12-grade provenance: if the papers are real it is history's first retrieval, and if not, it shows sworn-era claims amplifying uncorroborated paper — either way the chain of custody is the whole case.
